coredata.dc:title: Triboelectric electronic-skin based on graphene quantum dots for application in self-powered, smart, artificial fingers
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zhongwei
Wu, Chaoxing
Li, Fushan
Chen, Wei
Guo, Tailiang
Kim, Tae Whan
coredata.dc:description: The development of electronic-skin (e-skin) with artificial tactile-perception is crucial for emerging artificial-intelligence systems. However, considering the relatively simple function of existing e-skins, their performances still have much room for improvement. Here, a cuttable, transparent, stretchable, and lightweight e-skin that functions on the basis of the triboelectric effect is demonstrated. Well-designed micro-gaps are introduced to make the e-skin respond sensitively to various mechanical stimulations, including pressing, stretching, folding, and twisting. Ag nanowires coated with graphene quantum dots are employed as the electrode, as well as the friction layer, to increase the sensitivity to external mechanical stimulations. Self-powered, smart, artificial fingers with tactile sensation to monitor the actions of the fingers were fabricated to demonstrate the potential application of our newly developed e-skin. The architecture and the material system of the device demonstrated in this work will promote the development of human-machine interfaces and intelligent machines.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electronic-skin
Artificial finger
Graphene quantum dot
Self-powered electronics
Triboelectric nanogenerator

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U1605244 ) and by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2016YFB0401602 ). This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology ( 2016R1A2A1A05005502 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Basic structure and design of the e-skin. (a) Schematic of the e-skin and the graphene quantum dot-coated Ag NW (G-Ag NW) network. (b) 3D optical image of the patterned PDMS layer. The bottom panel presents the schematic cross section of the device. (c) SEM image of the Ag NWs. The inset is a high-magnification SEM image. (d) Low-magnification TEM image of G-Ag NWs. (e) High-magnification TEM image of G-Ag NWs. (f) Photograph of the transparent and lightweight e-skin. (g) Photographs of the e-skin with demonstrations of it undergoing different mechanical deformations, including twisting and stretching. (h) Photographs demonstrating that the as-fabricated e-skin is cuttable and that a big e-skin can be cut into smaller pieces.

Schematic illustration of the working mechanism of the e-skin. (a) Initial state of the e-skin. (b) PDMS comes into contact with G-Ag NWs under external mechanical stimulations, and electrons are transferred from the G-Ag NWs to the PDMS. (c) PDMS is separated from the G-Ag NWs due to release from the external mechanical stimulations. (d) Schematic illustration of the deformations of the e-skin under different pressures.

Output performances of e-skins with and without graphene QDs. (a)-(d) Output short-circuit current density of the e-skin without graphene QDs when the e-skin is being (a) pressed, (b) stretched, (c) folded, and (d) twisted. (e)-(h) Output short-circuit current density of the e-skin with graphene QDs when the e-skin is being (e) pressed, (f) stretched, (g) folded, and (h) twisted. (i) Illustration of the contact electrification process between PDMS and the Ag NWs. (j) Illustration of the contact electrification process between PDMS and the graphene quantum dot-coated Ag NW.

Variation in output current density as a function of the concentration of graphene QDs and the strength of mechanical stimulations. The e-skin is being (a) pressed, (b) stretched, (c) folded, and (d) twisted.

Application of the e-skin as smart artificial fingers. (a) Experimental design of the smart artificial fingers. The inset is a photograph of a small piece of e-skin attached to a human finger. (b) Time evolutions of the current densities from e-skins on the index finger and on the thumb. The panels beneath the plot present the corresponding finger actions. (c) Photograph of a keyboard being tapped with the smart artificial fingers. (d) Time evolution of the current density from the e-skin on the index finger when the index finger touches and taps a key.

head.ce:title: Triboelectric electronic-skin based on graphene quantum dots for application in self-powered, smart, artificial fingers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Optoelectronic Technology, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350116, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Optoelectronic Technology, Fuzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic and Computer Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-791, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronic and Computer Engineering, Hanyang University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of electronic-skin (e-skin) with artificial tactile-perception is crucial for emerging artificial-intelligence systems. However, considering the relatively simple function of existing e-skins, their performances still have much room for improvement. Here, a cuttable, transparent, stretchable, and lightweight e-skin that functions on the basis of the triboelectric effect is demonstrated. Well-designed micro-gaps are introduced to make the e-skin respond sensitively to various mechanical stimulations, including pressing, stretching, folding, and twisting. Ag nanowires coated with graphene quantum dots are employed as the electrode, as well as the friction layer, to increase the sensitivity to external mechanical stimulations. Self-powered, smart, artificial fingers with tactile sensation to monitor the actions of the fingers were fabricated to demonstrate the potential application of our newly developed e-skin. The architecture and the material system of the device demonstrated in this work will promote the development of human-machine interfaces and intelligent machines.

ce:section.ce:para: Promoted by the potential market value of emerging artificial intelligence, smart robots, artificial prostheses, and wearable electronics, the development of electronic-skin (e-skin) mimicking the many functions of human skin has attracted unprecedented attention because human skin is a key interface with the external environment [1–6]. As an important somatosensory system, human skin is extremely sensitive to the environment and can possess tactile perceptions for distinguishing external mechanical stimulations, such as pressing, touching, straining, and bending, and for generating bioelectric signals to nerve centers. Until now, various approaches for sensing touch/pressure stimulations have been utilized to develop e-skin. Especially, resistive sensors utilizing the resistance change and capacitive sensors utilizing the capacitance change have met great commercial success [7–10]. Worth noting is that the resistive sensors and the capacitive seniors are not self-powered electronics; rather, they need an external electrical supply to continuously detect the changes in resistance/capacitance. As a result, integration of a battery into the e-skin system is necessary. However, the presence of batteries makes the overall system bulkier, shortens its life-span, and limits applications in wearable electronics. The high dependence of e-skins on an external electrical supply is one of its essential differences from human skin, which can self-generate bioelectric signals according to changes in the external environment.

One of the key characteristics of e-skin, if it is to mimic human skin, is the ability to self-generate feedback electric signals according to changes in external mechanical stimulations. Thus, the concept of self-powered electronic devices becomes the key issue in the development of e-skin systems. A zinc-oxide nanostructure combined with a piezoelectric polymer has been used to realize flexible, self-powered e-skin [11]. Recently, triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) and related self-powered technology, which are based on triboelectrification and electrostatic induction, have been proven to be a promising approach to realizing self-powered mechanical sensors due to their high efficiency for converting vibration energy to electrical energy [12–15]. Especially, flexible triboelectric devices that hold promising in e-skin applications have been realized using graphene [16], electronic textiles [17], conductive paper [18], and conductive polymer composites [19]. Several kinds of e-skin have been successfully demonstrated to perceive external mechanical stimulations. For example, a TENG-based e-skin was developed to detect static finger gestures and dynamic motion [20]. A fully self-sufficient and body-conformable e-skin system that could generate electricity from external touch was realized by utilizing a skin-like triboelectric nanogenerator [21]. Graphene tribotronics for interfacing electronics to environmental touch stimuli have been developed and have been shown to exhibit excellent touch-sensing performance [22]. Even though the development of TENG-based e-skins has made great progress, the performances of e-skins still have much room for improvement. Worth noting is that human skin can distinguish various mechanical stimulations, such as pressing, touching, straining, and bending. However, most reported e-skins based on a planar structure can only produce electrical outputs in response to external presses and touches. The realization of strain and bending sensing is of importance for the development of a fully functional e-skin. On the other hand, astonishingly, human skin can judge the stimulation strength, which capability has never been reported for e-skins. These considerations show that the development of e-skin that can establish a highly sensitive interaction with diverse external mechanical stimulations, as a multi-mechanical mode sensor system, is necessary to ensure continuous progress in the emerging field of artificial smart electronics.

In this work, two approaches are employed to solving the problems of the existing plane-structure-based e-skins and to enhance the performance of e-skins: (1) Well-designed micro-gaps are introduced into the membrane structure to make the e-skin respond sensitively in real time to changes in pressure. (2) Graphene quantum dot-coated Ag nanowires (G-Ag NWs) are employed as the electrode, as well as the friction layer, which helps to generate a much stronger output and increase the sensitivity to the mechanical stimulations. Based on these designs, a transparent, stretchable, and lightweight TENG-based self-powered e-skin that can generate electricity from not only touch but also pressing, twisting, stretching, and folding were demonstrated in this work. Furthermore, the electrical outputs of our e-skin are highly sensitive to the magnitude of the pressure and the degree of deformation. The simple structure of our e-skin allows its size and shape to be modified without adversely affecting its ability to function properly. Finally, we show that the e-skin, with its excellent flexibility, can fully conform to a finger and can act as a self-powered mechanical sensor with tactile sensation, which holds promise for potential applications in smart artificial fingers and smart robots.

The flow of the process used to fabricate the e-skin is depicted in Fig. S1. The fabrication process starts with the preparation of patterned polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) films. Firstly, a photoresist film with a thickness of 5 µm is spin-coated on a clean glass substrate. Then, a uniform line array with a linewidth of 100 µm and a gap of 100 µm is fabricated by using the traditional photolithography method. The patterned photoresist acts as the mold for fabricating the patterned PDMS films. In the preparation of the patterned PDMS films, a PDMS elastomer and a cross-linker (SYLGARD 184, Dow Corning) are mixed in a weight ratio of 10:1. After a degassing process under vacuum, the elastomer mixture is spin-coated on the patterned photoresist-coated glass at 300 rpm, followed by annealing at 120 °C for 1 h. Finally, the free-standing PDMS film with the groove pattern is peeled off the glass substrate.

The G-Ag NWs are deposited on the PDMS film separately. The original surface of the PDMS is hydrophobic. Thus, the patterned surface of the PDMS film is treated with an oxygen plasma to increase the hydrophilicity. The Ag NWs (CST-NW-S40, Suzhou ColdStones Technology) are blade coated on the bottom of the grooves, followed by annealing at 90 °C for 20 min. The Ag NW network acts as a bottom electrode array. Similarly, the Ag NW network is treated with an oxygen plasma to increase the hydrophilicity before the deposition of the graphene QDs. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs, XF215, Nanjing XFNANO Materials TECH Co., Ltd) are blade coated on the surface of the Ag NW film, followed by annealing at 90 °C for 20 min. As a result, the graphene QDs are attached to the Ag NWs through van der Waals' forces. Finally, the as-prepared PDMS/Ag NW network/graphene QDs substrate is covered by another free-standing PDMS film with the same pattern of Ag NWs, which acts as the top electrode array. Note that the top and the bottom electrode arrays are aligned in the vertical direction. Finally, another free-standing thin PDMS film, acting as the encapsulation film, is placed on the stacked PDMS substrate to form a fully closed structure. The two edges of the device are connected to copper conducting wires by using conductive adhesive tape.

The surface morphologies of the patterned PDMS were characterized by using an 3D measuring laser microscope (OLYMPUS LEXT OLS4100, OLYMPUS). Ag NWs were characterized by using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI Quanta 400 FEG) and an atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker multimode 8). Ag NWs coated with graphene QDs were characterized by using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20). For the electrical output measurements of the e-skins, a Keithley 4200 semiconductor characterization system was utilized to measure the short-circuit current. The transmittance of the e-skin was measured by using an UV−vis spectroscopy spectrophotometer (UV-3600, SHIMADZU). In the demonstration of smart artificial fingers, a large piece of e-skin was cut into smaller pieces with areas of about 0.4 cm2 each, and these smaller pieces were attached to fingertips. Then, those small pieces of e-skin were connected to a Keithley 4200 semiconductor characterization system to collect the electrical signals produced by the actions of the fingers.

The basic components of a TENG-based device are two friction layers that have distinctly different triboelectric characteristics. When the two layers come into contact with each other under external mechanical stimulations, triboelectric charges with opposite polarities are generated and distributed on the internal surfaces of the two layers. When the opposite triboelectric charges are separated and form dipole moments, an electric potential difference is established between the two electrodes, which can cause electrons to flow and generate output electrical signals. This is the basic operating principle of TENG-based devices. For most TENG-based e-skins, the single-electrode operation mode is usually employed. In that case, the human skin is acting as one of the friction layers, and the polymer layer of the e-skin is acting as the other friction layer. As a result, the device can only detect whether or not the human skin touches the e-skin; the strength of touch cannot be measured exactly [20–22]. To make the e-skin more sensitive to the strength of the external mechanical stimulation, we introduce well-aligned micro-gaps into the membrane structure. A schematic of the structure of our e-skin is shown in Fig. 1a.

The detailed fabrication processes are described in the Experimental section and Fig. S1. To fabricate micro-gaps in the membrane structure, we first prepare patterned PDMS films with well-aligned groove patterns. A patterned photoresist acting as a mold is then fabricated by using the traditional photolithography method, followed by uniformly spin-coating with a liquid PDMS elastomer and a cross-linker. The as-prepared PDMS layer has groove pattern features that are the inverse of those on the mold. The optical 3D image of the as-prepared PDMS layer shows a uniform groove array with a depth of 5 µm, a width of 100 µm, and a period of 200 µm (Fig. 1b). As the bottom panel of Fig. 1b schematically shows, the grooves act as micro-gaps in the e-skin. After the preparation of the patterned PDMS layer, Ag NW networks and QDs are deposited on the bottoms of the grooves, thereby forming graphene QD-coated Ag NW (G-Ag NW) electrodes. The as-prepared PDMS film with G-Ag NW electrodes is then covered by another free-standing PDMS film with a pattern that is the same as that of the Ag NW films that are acting as the top electrode array. Note that the top and the bottom electrode arrays are aligned in the vertical direction. Finally, another free-standing thin PDMS film is used to form a fully closed structure. Fig. 1c shows a top-view SEM image of the Ag NW network deposited on PDMS; the Ag NW network is seen to be uniformly formed by using blade coating. Fig. 1d and e show TEM images of the G-Ag NW network and of graphene QDs on a Ag nanowire, respectively. The graphene QDs with diameters of 20–30 nm are seen to be attached uniformly to the surfaces of the Ag NWs. Because of the simple fabrication process, the G-Ag NW electrodes have high repeatability, as shown in Fig. S2.

Ag NWs are utilized in this work due to their advantages of low cost, easy synthesis, and high transparency. Furthermore, the Ag NW networks show outstanding flexibility and stability with high transparency to visible light, all of which make the fabrication of flexible, stretchable, and transparent e-skin possible. The relatively rough surface of the Ag NW networks is especially important for triboelectronic nanogenerators, in which increased contact area is beneficial. Of particular note is that graphene QDs are bio-friendly and will not harm humans or damage the environment. Furthermore, the addition of graphene quantum dots can significantly improve the output of the e-skin due to the efficient electron transfer from the Ag NWs to the graphene QDs, which will be discussed later.

Large-area transparent e-skin films (10 ×10 cm2) can be fabricated by using the above method. The measured transmittances and absorptances of the e-skin and of PDMS are presented in Fig. S3. As shown in inset of Fig. 1f, the e-skin is ultra-lightweight with an areal density of approximately 130 g/m2, which makes it suitable for applications in portable and wearable electronics. Because the e-skins are fabricated using a low-temperature all-solution method, they can be easily scaled up as desired, suggesting large-scale, cost-effective, and industrial-friendly manufacturability. The good tear strength of PDMS and the network structure of the Ag NW electrode enable the e-skin to retain functionality and stretchability even after having undergone severe mechanical stretching. The photograph in the top panel of Fig. 1g demonstrates the high stretchability of the as-fabricated e-skin. The e-skin can easily endure strains up to ≈ 140% (bottom panel of Fig. 1g). More discussion about the influence of strain on the electrical performance will be presented below.

Even though several kinds of TENG-based e-skins have been developed, very few investigations on cuttable TENG-based e-skin have been reported. Conventional TENG-based e-skins cannot be scaled-down by directly cutting the as-fabricated devices. Possible reasons are that the components are too expensive to be discarded and that the cutting might irreversibly damage internal electronic circuits. However, our e-skin can be optionally cut to a custom shape and size without any adverse effect on its operation because of its simple structure, which is shown in Fig. 1h. The e-skins demonstrated in the following sections were obtained by cutting a large e-skin into smaller ones.

The basic working mechanisms of the e-skin are schematically described in Fig. 2, which is based on a combination of triboelectrification and electrostatic induction. In the initial state, static charge does not exist on either the surface of PDMS or the G-Ag NW network (Fig. 2a). Because of the distinctly different triboelectric characteristics of PDMS and the G-Ag NW network, electrons will transfer from the G-Ag NW network to the PDMS surface when the two layers come into contact with each other as the result of an external mechanical stimulation (Fig. 2b). Thus, the two layers are charged with opposite static charges. After the external mechanical stimulation is released, the two layers separate from each other, leading to a potential difference between the top Ag NW electrode and the bottom G-Ag NW electrode. As a result, electrons driven by the triboelectric potential difference will transfer via an external circuit (Fig. 2c). This process can cause an output voltage/current in an external load. When negative charges on the PDMS are equilibrated by the induced positive charges, no output signals can be observed. Once the negatively-charged PDMS surface comes into contact with the G-Ag NW network again under an external mechanical stimulation, the density of the induced positive charges on the top Ag NW network decreases. Thus, a current flows from the top Ag NW electrode to the bottom G-Ag NW electrode. The periodic contact and separation between the PDMS and the G-Ag NW network caused by a periodic external mechanical stimulation produce periodic output electrical signals from the e-skin.

Note that the elastic micro-walls in our e-skin design play an important role. Firstly, the micro-walls acting as spacers support the PDMS, keeping it separated from the G-Ag NW network. Under external pressing, the bottom part (the PDMS surface) of the middle PDMS layer will come into contact with and press against the G-Ag NW electrodes of the lower PDMS layer so that the two friction components can function. Once the pressing is released, both the PDMS surface and the G-Ag NW electrodes will automatically separate. Furthermore, due to the existence of the elastic micro-walls, the contact area and the number of generated static charges depend greatly on the pressing strength (pressure). With these features of the structure, the e-skin can sensitively respond in real time to the strength of the mechanical stimulation by generating electrical signals with different amplitudes.

The generated open-circuit voltage (V OC) can be expressed as:
(1)
V
OC
=
σ d
ε
0
,
where σ is the triboelectric charge density, d is the distance between the two electrodes, and ε o is the vacuum permittivity [23]. According to Eq. (1), an increased σ, which is strongly related to the contact surface area between the friction layers, can cause a higher open-circuit voltage. As schematically shown in Fig. 2d, due to the introduction of micro-gaps in the e-skin, a stronger mechanical stimulation can cause a greater deformation of the PDMS layers, thereby increasing the contact surface area between the PDMS and the G-Ag NW network and producing more surface triboelectric charges during the friction process.

Furthermore, the generated current (I) can be calculated as follows:
(2)
I
=
C
∂ V
∂ t
+
V
∂ C
∂ t
,
where C is the total capacitance of the device, and V is the voltage drop between the two electrodes [24]. The first part refers to the variation in the voltage drop between the two electrodes caused by the triboelectric charges, and the second part refers to the variation in the capacitance caused by the change in the distance between the two electrodes. Thus, according to Eqs. (1) and (2), a larger voltage variation and a larger capacitance variation can be produced under a stronger mechanical stimulation. This is the basic principle of our e-skin, which is very sensitive to the magnitude of the stimulation and to the degree of deformation.

Due to the excellent stretchability and flexibility of our e-skin, it can operate under various deformations without mechanical failure. The measured short-circuit current densities generated by the device without graphene QDs are shown in Fig. 3a-d. (The measured open-circuit voltages are present in Fig. S4). Under different mechanical stimulations, including pressing, stretching, folding, and twisting, the device can generate output currents, indicative of possible applications of artificial skin for feeling various mechanical stimulations. However, as shown in Fig. 3a and b, the currents generated under pressure and strain are relatively low, and increasing the pressure and the strain can only enlarge the output current slightly. Moreover, even though the device is more sensitive to folding and twisting (Fig. 3c and d), the generated currents are still low, which might complicate the required external driving circuits. As we discussed above, the micro-gaps play a key role in the sensitivity of e-skins. However, in the micro-gaps, the two triboelectric layers (PDMS and Ag NW network) have only a relatively small displacement even when they are fully separated. The displacement is too small to induce a high electrical potential difference between the two electrodes; thus, the electrical potential is too small to drive electrons efficiently through the external load under a short-circuit condition (Eq. (1)). This is the reason for the relatively low output current from the device without graphene QDs.

According to Eq. (1), increasing the triboelectric charge density (σ) is an approach for enhancing the electrical output of TENG-based devices. Typically, appropriate friction materials with markedly different polarities can be used to increase the density of triboelectric charges [25]. Introduction of electron traps into the common friction layer is another efficient method to enhance the electrical output [26,27]. In this work, for the first time, we use graphene QDs to modify the Ag NWs, with an aim to enhance the electron transfer process between the Ag NWs and PDMS. A graphene QD solution with a concentration of 10 mg/ml is blade coated on the Ag NW network, and the short-circuit currents are measured. As shown in Fig. 3e, the output currents from the e-skin with graphene QDs are enhanced dramatically. Typically, when a pressure of 10 N is applied to the e-skin, an output short-circuit current density of ~10 mA/cm2 is produced, which is approximately 20 times larger than that of the device without graphene QDs. When the applied pressure is released, the induced short-circuit current has the same magnitude, but with reversed sign. The generated output current increases linearly with increasing applied pressure in our testing range (Fig. 3e). In the case of pressure stimulation, the generated current density increases from ~10 mA/cm2 at 10 N to ~20 mA/cm2 at 30 N. Thus, increased pressure leads to increased current output because the triboelectric charge density increases when the contact surface area is increased, which is the case when the graphene QDs are introduced. Similarly, under strain and folding stimulations, the e-skin with graphene QDs produces electrical outputs that are approximately 20 times higher than those of the device without the QDs, as shown in Fig. 3f and g. In our testing range, the generated output current increases with increasing degrees of strain and folding. As shown in Fig. 3g, the e-skin with graphene QDs exhibits an ~3 times higher output under twisting than the device without graphene QDs, while keeping its high sensitivities to different stimulations. The measured open-circuit voltages are present in Fig. S4. The results confirm the high sensitivities of our e-skin to pressure, strain, folding, and twisting. Based on the robust relationship between the current output and the strengths of the stimulations, our devices hold promise for potential applications in smart artificial skin with tactile-perception behavior.

According to the electron transfer mechanism, when two different kinds of materials are in contact, electrons will transfer from one to the other due to the difference in the effective work functions [28]. Schematic diagrams of the energy bands for the devices are described in Fig. 3i and j to explain the possible mechanisms for the output enhancement related to the introduction of graphene QDs. Fig. 3i illustrates the charge transfer between the Ag NWs and the PDMS layer in contact electrification, which is the principle for the generation of triboelectric charges on the PDMS surface and image charges on the Ag NW network. Although the band gap of PDMS is large, surface states exist within the bandgap [29]. The highest filled surface energy states of PDMS are normally below the Fermi level of Ag (Fig. 3i-I). When Ag NWs come into contact with PDMS, electrons will flow from the Ag NWs to the PDMS surface to fill the surface states. In that case, the highest filled surface energy states of the PDMS are as high as the Fermi level of Ag (Fig. 3i-II). However, when the two materials start separating from each other, a local electric field will build up due to the existence of electrons on the PDMS surface and positive image charges on the Ag NW side. The strength of this locally built-up electric field is determined by the separation distance. Thus, some of the electrons on the PDMS surface will flow back to the Ag NWs. The levels of the highest filled surface energy states of the charged PDMS will decrease (Fig. 3i-III), as will the total density of triboelectric charges on the PDMS surface. As a result, the electrical output of the triboelectric device is limited.

In the case of G-Ag NWs, the electrons will transfer from the Ag NWs to the surfaces of the graphene QDs to fill the surface states of those QDs (Fig. 3j-I). When the G-Ag NWs come into contact with the PDMS layer, electrons will flow from the Ag NWs to the graphene QDs and then to the PDMS. In that case, the highest filled surface energy states of the PMDS and the graphene QDs are as high as the Fermi level of Ag (Fig. 3j-II). Once the G-Ag NWs and the PDMS layer are separated, the locally built-up electric field will cause some of the electrons on the PDMS surface to flow back to the Ag NWs. However, the existence of electrons in the surface energy states of the graphene QDs will reduce the strength of the locally built-up electric field. Thus, the total density of triboelectric charges on the PDMS surface in the device with graphene QDs is higher than that of the PDMS surface in the device without graphene QDs (Fig. 3j-II). The electrical output of the e-skin with graphene QDs is higher.

To further check the proposed mechanism, we first change the concentration of graphene QDs. A reasonable assumption is that increasing the concentration of graphene QDs blade-coated on the Ag NW network will result in more graphene QDs being attached to the Ag NWs. Therefore, as is expected from the proposed model, the generated output current should increase proportionally with the number of graphene QDs. Fig. 4a-d and Figs. S5–8 show the variations in the output current density with respect to the concentration of graphene QDs and the strengths of the mechanical stimulations. The output current shows a strong dependence on the concentration of graphene QDs. In general, in our testing range, the output currents generated under pressure, strain, folding, and twisting stimulations increase with increasing concentration of graphene QDs. For example, as the graphene QD concentration is increased from 0 to 10 mg/ml, the output current density under a pressure of 10 N increases from 0.42 to 10.63 mA/m2 (Fig. 4a) and that under a pressure of 30 N increases from 1.22 to 17.7 mA/m2. Similarly, compared to mechanical stimulations with small amplitudes, such as small strain, folding, and twisting, the effect of the graphene-QD concentration on the currents generated under stronger stimulations is remarkable, as shown in Fig. 4b-d. The largest output currents are observed when the graphene QD concentration is 10 mg/ml. The results can mainly be attributed to the following factors: The Ag NW network is formed by stacking randomly crossed Ag NWs together, and the surfaces of the Ag NWs in the Ag NW network are rough. As a result, under a smaller mechanical stimulation, only the upper graphene QDs in the crossing regions of the Ag NWs will come into contact with the PDMS layer and contribute to the output enhancement. Due to the excellent elasticity of PDMS, the PDMS surface easily deforms and can come into increased contact with the lower graphene QDs when a higher mechanical stimulation is applied. Therefore, the number of graphene QDs contributing to the output signal is obviously increased. This strong graphene-QD dependence of the produced output supports the proposed mechanism.

Furthermore, we compare the surface morphology of the Ag NW network with that of the graphene QD-coated Ag NW network (Fig. S9). The surface roughness of the graphene QD-coated Ag NW network formed from the graphene QD solution (10 mg/ml) is larger than that of the pure Ag NW network. Thus, at that QD concentration, the contact area between the graphene QD-coated Ag NW network and the PDMS film should be increased, which might contribute slightly to the enhancement of the device's output. However, The surface roughness of the pure Ag NW network is almost the same as that of the graphene QD-coated Ag NW network samples prepared with graphene QD concentrations of 2, 4, 6, and 8 mg/ml. Therefore, the obviously enhanced output presented in Fig. 4 should not be attributed primarily to a variation in the morphology of the Ag NW network.

The high sensitivities to mechanical stimulations of our e-skin make possible its use in smart artificial fingers with tactile perception. For traditional pressure sensors, an external power supply is necessary if real-time sensitivities are to be achieved. From the view that the TENG-based devices can work without an extra power source, our e-skin can be assembled smartly, offering greater convenience for artificial fingers applied in smart robots or artificial prostheses. Moreover, the total cost of the e-skin can be greatly reduced due to its advantages of large-scale, cost-effective, and industrial-friendly manufacturability. To demonstrate the application of our e-skin in smart artificial fingers, we cut a large e-skin into small pieces, which we attach to fingertips (inset of Fig. 5a). When the hand holds the target objects or the fingers touch the target objects, electrical signals are produced, as schematically shown in Fig. 5a. Furthermore, the magnitude of the finger pressure and the motion of the fingers can be detected.

For example, one e-skin is attached to the tip of the index finger, and another is attached to the tip of the thumb to detect the motions of those fingers. The current signals produced by the two e-skins responding to the actions of the fingers show different characteristics, as shown in Fig. 5b and Video S1. The e-skin on the index finger senses the action of the index finger while the e-skin on the thumb senses the action of the thumb. In the initial state, these skins produce no electrical signals. When a weight is lifted vertically by the index finger and the thumb, a current pulse is produced from each of the two e-skins. When the weight is wobbled, a continuous current pulse can be detected from each of the two e-skins. When the weight is held horizontally on the thumb and is wobbled, the e-skin on the thumb produces an output current stronger than that produced by the e-skin on the index finger. In this case, the thumb senses a stronger pressure, and the related e-skin can sense the change in the pressure. When the weight is held horizontally on the index finger, the e-skin on the index finger produces a stronger output current. Based on the detected currents, the basic actions of the fingers can be recognized easily. We also investigate the outputs of the e-skins on the fingers when tapping a keyboard. Typically, recording the time sequence when the keys are tapped is easy. However, when our e-skins are used, the magnitudes of the induced current can contain rich information about the force of the typewriting, as shown in Fig. 5c, d and Video S2. When the fingers lightly touch the keys, current pulses with relatively low magnitudes are produced. However, when the fingers tap the keys, stronger output currents can be produced. Thus, the actions of the fingers can be simultaneously recorded and monitored by collecting the output current signals. Although this work only demonstrates the feasibility of using human fingers with e-skins for monitoring the activities of fingers, the use of our e-skin for real-time action monitoring is expected to be extended to include artificial limbs, mechanical arms, intelligent robots, etc.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2018.04.059.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video 1, Video 2.
Video 1
E-skins for the detection of finger motions. The basic actions of the fingers can be easily recognized based on the detected currents.
Video 2
Outputs of the e-skins on the fingers when a keyboard is being tapped. The magnitudes of the induced current may contain rich information about the force of the typewriting.

In summary, a cuttable, transparent, stretchable, and lightweight e-skin on the basis of the triboelectric effect is demonstrated. The employment of graphene QDs-coated Ag NWs network increases the triboelectric charge density and dramatically enhances the electrical outputs. The sandwich structured e-skin with micro-gaps in the membrane sensitively responds various extra mechanical stimulations, including press, stretch, folding, and twisting. By employing the e-skin, self-powered smart artificial fingers with tactile sensation are demonstrated to monitor the action of the fingers. This novel material system of graphene QDs-coated Ag NWs network might offer effective approaches to designing wearable self-powered electronic devices with high electrical output performance. These findings also provide a promising strategy to achieve high performance human-machine interfaces and intelligent machines.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1605244) and by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFB0401602). This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (2016R1A2A1A05005502).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2018.04.059.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ultra-lightweight design for imperceptible plastic electronics
Skin-inspired highly stretchable and conformable matrix network for multifunctional sensing
Electronic skin with multifunction sensors based on thermosensation
A dual-organic-transistor-based tactile-perception system with signal-processing functionality
Self-powered implantable electronic-skin for in situ analysis of urea/uric-acid in body fluids and the potential applications in real-time kidney-disease diagnosis
Fabrication of novel transparent touch sensing device via drop-on-demand inkjet printing technique
Flexible, cuttable, and self-waterproof bending strain sensors using microcracked gold nanofilms@paper substrate
A breathable and screen-printed pressure sensor based on nanofiber membranes for electronic skins
Carbonized silk nanofiber membrane for transparent and sensitive electronic skin
A flexible self-powered T-ZnO/PVDF/fabric electronic-skin with multifunctions of tactile-perception, atmosphere-detection and self-clean
Toward the blue energy dream by triboelectric nanogenerator network
Flexible nanogenerators for energy harvesting and self-powered electronics
Energy harvesters for wearable and stretchable electronics: from flexibility to stretchability
Achieving ultrahigh triboelectric charge density for efficient energy harvesting
Conformal, graphene-based triboelectric nanogenerator for self-powered wearable electronics
Wearable electricity generators fabricated utilizing transparent electronic textiles based on polyester/Ag nanowires/graphene core–shell nanocomposites
Ultrasoft and cuttable paper-based triboelectric nanogenerators for mechanical energy harvesting
A novel soft metal-polymer composite for multidirectional pressure energy harvesting
An intelligent skin based self-powered finger motion sensor integrated with triboelectric nanogenerator
Electric eel-skin-inspired mechanically durable and super-Stretchable nanogenerator for deformable power source and fully autonomous conformable electronic-skin applications
Graphene tribotronics for electronic skin and touch screen applications
Studying about applied force and the output performance of sliding-mode triboelectric nanogenerators
Transparent triboelectric nanogenerators and self-powered pressure sensors based on micropatterned plastic films
Reduced graphene-oxide acting as electron-trapping sites in the friction layer for giant triboelectric enhancement
Enhanced triboelectric nanogenerators based on MoS2 monolayer nanocomposites acting as electron-acceptor layers
Manipulating nanoscale contact electrification by an applied electric field

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhong-Wei Xu received his bachelor's degree in physics from Northwest Normal University in 2015. Currently, he is pursuing his Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Fu-Shan Li at the College of Physics and Information Engineering, Fuzhou University, People’s Republic of China. His research interest in his Ph.D. study is mainly focused on the preparation of functional nanomaterials and the determination of their correlative properties.

Dr. Chaoxing Wu received his Ph.D. degree from the College of Physics and Information Engineering, Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China, in 2015. He is working as a research assistant professor at Hanyang University, Republic of Korea. His current research interests include flexible and wearable smart electronics based on low-dimensional nanomaterials.

Professor Li Fushan received his Ph.D. degree from the School of Physics, Peking University, in 2005. From September 2006 to February 2009, he worked as a research professor at Hanyang University, Republic of Korea, and from March 2009 to October 2009, he was a research fellow at Nanyang Technological University, Singapore. He has been with the School of Physics and Information Engineering, Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China, as a full professor since October 2009. Professor Li's research specialty is nanoelectronic devices and fabrication technology; he is especially interested in the optoelectronic properties of nanomaterials for applications in next-generation photovoltaic, memory, and light-emitting devices.

Wei Chen received his master of engineering degree in microelectronics and solid state electronics in 2007 from Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China. He is presently a Ph.D. student in microelectronics and solid state electronics at Fuzhou University. His research interest comprises the development of new nanomaterials for applications in electrochemical sensors and optoelectronic devices.

Professor Tailiang Guo graduated from Fuzhou University, People's Republic of China, in 1986. He has worked at Fuzhou University since 1986 and was promoted to full professor in 2008. He has made important progress in research on key materials and fabrication techniques for low-light-level night-vision devices, field emission displays, and high-light backlights for liquid crystal displays. The current research topics of his group include display technology, optoelectronic technology, and energy-related technology.

Prof. Taewhan Kim received his Ph.D. degree from the Department of Physics, State University of New York at Buffalo, in 1989. Currently, he is the HYU Distinguished Professor in the Department of Electronic Engineering, Hanyang University, Republic of Korea. Professor Kim's research specialty is nanodevices and nanomaterials for investigations of both fundamental physics and promising applications in electronic and optoelectronic devices. His current interests are focused on the fabrication of next-generation nonvolatile memory devices, organic light-emitting devices, solar cells, and nanogenerators.
